description: The cardinal direction opposite 'nitho' (north) — the open expanse toward which the still axis faces. One of the two coined cardinal anchors; east and west are expressed compositionally from the sun's path: 'sorae thorui' (sun's beginning, east) and 'sorae lumae' (sun's end, west).
sound_symbolism: The flowing 'ro' sets out in motion, the grounding 'nu' passes through depth, and the open 'a' arrives at wide horizon — an expanse unrolling away from the fixed point. Deliberately shares almost nothing with its partner 'nitho' (north), or with 'lawe'/'kuri' (left/right), so the four directions can never be confused.
grammatical_notes: A noun naming the direction. Before another noun it modifies by position: 'ronua ruela' (the south path). With prepositions: 'wea ronua' (southward), 'lue ronua' (from the south). Value-neutral by design: south carries no sense of down, heat, or lesserness — those associations are hemisphere- and map-relative, which Phi declines to encode.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: No direction is 'down' or lesser in Phi. The compass is a circle of equals, and its words refuse the map-maker's hierarchy.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: The direction the sky-watcher faces away from the axis — the other half of the oldest compass, complete with two words and the sun's own path.

tags.nature: The open expanse of the turning sky
tags.spatial: Cardinal direction opposite the axis point
